title: CIL System — Advanced Mock 13 (Conceptual)
pattern: CIL MT — 200 MCQ · 2 papers · 1 mark each · no negative marking

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: As per Income Tax Department data (June 2026), India's net direct tax collections in early FY27 rose by about what percentage year-on-year?
questions.solution: Net direct tax collections rose ~14.6% YoY to about Rs 5.21 trillion.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: In 2026, which country became the first Central Asian member of the New Development Bank (NDB)?
questions.solution: Uzbekistan became the first Central Asian nation to join the BRICS-backed NDB.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which India-born theoretical physicist won the 2025 Wolf Prize in Physics?
questions.solution: Rajasthan-born physicist Jainendra K. Jain received the 2025 Wolf Prize in Physics.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which institution ranked first in the QS World University Rankings 2027 (released June 2026)?
questions.solution: Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) ranked first in QS World University Rankings 2027.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: In June 2026, which Indian jurist was elected a Judge of the International Tribunal for the Law of the Sea (ITLOS)?
questions.solution: Indian jurist Bimal N. Patel was elected to ITLOS in June 2026.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: In 2026, PM Narendra Modi received the highest national award of which country?
questions.solution: PM Modi was conferred Slovakia's highest national award in 2026.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Exercise KHAAN QUEST 2026, in which an Indian Army contingent participated, was held in which country?
questions.solution: The multinational Exercise KHAAN QUEST 2026 was held in Mongolia.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Who was named the new Chairman of GIC Re in June 2026?
questions.solution: The government named Hitesh Joshi as the new Chairman of GIC Re.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: In 2026, ONGC was assigned to develop a new strategic petroleum reserve (SPR) facility at which location?
questions.solution: ONGC was tasked with building a new underground crude oil SPR cavern at Mangaluru, Karnataka.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The India–UK CETA and Social Security Agreement were reported to take effect from which date?
questions.solution: Reports indicated the India–UK CETA would take effect from July 15, 2026.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which vitamin is produced by the human body on exposure to sunlight?
questions.solution: Sunlight triggers Vitamin D synthesis in the skin.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The 'Maharatna' status was granted to Coal India Limited in which decade?
questions.solution: CIL was conferred Maharatna status in 2011 (the 2010s).

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The largest coalfield in India by reserves is the:
questions.solution: Jharia in Jharkhand holds major prime coking coal reserves.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which Indian state has the longest coastline?
questions.solution: Gujarat has the longest coastline among Indian states.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The headquarters of Coal India Limited (CIL) is located in which city?
questions.solution: Coal India Limited is headquartered in Kolkata, West Bengal.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which is the largest coal-producing state in India in recent years?
questions.solution: Chhattisgarh has been India's largest coal-producing state, ahead of Odisha and Jharkhand.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Coal India Limited operates under which Union Ministry?
questions.solution: CIL functions under the Ministry of Coal, Government of India.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which subsidiary of CIL is the single largest coal-producing company?
questions.solution: South Eastern Coalfields Limited (SECL) is among CIL's largest producing subsidiaries.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: What is the capital of Australia?
questions.solution: Canberra is the capital of Australia, not Sydney or Melbourne.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: What is the capital of Canada?
questions.solution: Ottawa is the national capital of Canada.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Mount Kilimanjaro, the highest mountain in Africa, is located in which country?
questions.solution: Mount Kilimanjaro lies in north-eastern Tanzania.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which is the largest planet in the Solar System?
questions.solution: Jupiter is the largest planet by both mass and diameter.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which planet is known as the Red Planet?
questions.solution: Iron oxide on its surface gives Mars a reddish appearance.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The SI unit of electric current is the:
questions.solution: The ampere (A) is the SI base unit of electric current.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which gas is most abundant in Earth's atmosphere?
questions.solution: Nitrogen makes up about 78% of the atmosphere by volume.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A trader marks goods 40% above cost, then gives a 20% discount. His net profit percentage is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Net selling factor is the markup factor times the discount factor; profit% is that factor reduced by 1.
**Formula.** $f=\left(1+\tfrac{m}{100}\right)\left(1-\tfrac{d}{100}\right)$
**Given.** Markup m = 40%, discount d = 20%.
**Working.** $f=\left(1+\tfrac{40}{100}\right)\left(1-\tfrac{20}{100}\right)=1.12$.
**Answer.** Profit $=(f-1)\times100=12\%$.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: Two trains 180 m and 100 m long move in opposite directions at 60 km/h and 45 km/h. The time to cross each other is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Opposite directions add speeds; convert km/h to m/s with the factor 5/18, then divide the combined length by the relative speed.
**Formula.** $t=\tfrac{L_1+L_2}{(s_1+s_2)\times\frac{5}{18}}$
**Given.** L₁ = 180 m, L₂ = 100 m, s₁ = 60 km/h, s₂ = 45 km/h.
**Working.** Relative $=(60+45)\times\tfrac{5}{18}=29.17$ m/s; $t=\tfrac{180+100}{29.17}$.
**Answer.** $t=9.6$ s.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A pipe fills a tank in 8 h, but with a leak it takes 9 h. The leak alone can empty the full tank in:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The leak's rate is the fill-rate without the leak minus the slower fill-rate with the leak.
**Formula.** $T=\tfrac{ab}{b-a}$
**Given.** Fill without leak a = 8 h; with leak b = 9 h.
**Working.** Leak rate $=\tfrac1{8}-\tfrac1{9}$; emptying time $=\tfrac{8\times9}{9-8}$.
**Answer.** $T=72$ h.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The difference between compound and simple interest on Rs 12000 at 12% per annum for 2 years is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Over 2 years the gap between compound and simple interest is the first year's interest-on-interest.
**Formula.** $CI-SI=P\left(\tfrac{r}{100}\right)^2$
**Given.** P = Rs 12000, r = 12% p.a., t = 2 yr.
**Working.** $=12000\left(\tfrac{12}{100}\right)^2$.
**Answer.** $CI-SI=$ Rs 172.8.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A invests Rs 4000 for 6 months and B invests Rs 8000 for 6 months. Out of a total profit of Rs 6000, A's share is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Profit is split in the ratio of capital × time invested by each partner.
**Formula.** $\text{share}_A=P\cdot\tfrac{x\,m_A}{x\,m_A+y\,m_B}$
**Given.** A: Rs 4000×6 mo; B: Rs 8000×6 mo; profit Rs 6000.
**Working.** Ratio $=24000:48000$; share$_A=6000\times\tfrac{24000}{72000}$.
**Answer.** A's share $=$ Rs 2000.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The angle between the hour and minute hands of a clock at 4:20 is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The hour hand sweeps 30° per hour and 0.5° per minute, the minute hand 6° per minute; take the smaller of the two angles.
**Formula.** $\theta=\left|30H-5.5M\right|$
**Given.** H = 4, M = 20.
**Working.** $=\left|30\times4-5.5\times20\right|=10^\circ$; smaller $=\min(10,360-10)$.
**Answer.** $\theta=10^\circ$.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A vessel holds 80 L of milk. 8 L is drawn out and replaced with water; this is repeated 2 times. The milk remaining is about:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Each draw-and-replace keeps the fraction $(1-x/V)$ of milk; after n repeats this fraction is raised to the n-th power.
**Formula.** $M=V\left(1-\tfrac{x}{V}\right)^{n}$
**Given.** V = 80 L, x = 8 L, n = 2.
**Working.** $=80\left(1-\tfrac{8}{80}\right)^{2}$.
**Answer.** $M\approx 64.8$ L.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A boat covers 55 km downstream in 5 h and the same 55 km upstream in 11 h. Its speed in still water is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Downstream speed adds the stream; upstream subtracts it. Still-water speed is the average of the two.
**Formula.** $u=\tfrac12\left[(b+s)+(b-s)\right]$
**Given.** Downstream 55 km in 5 h; upstream 55 km in 11 h.
**Working.** Downstream $=\tfrac{55}{5}=11$ km/h, upstream $=\tfrac{55}{11}=5$ km/h.
**Answer.** Still-water speed $u=\tfrac{11+5}{2}=8$ km/h.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A trader marks goods 60% above cost, then gives a 25% discount. His net profit percentage is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Net selling factor is the markup factor times the discount factor; profit% is that factor reduced by 1.
**Formula.** $f=\left(1+\tfrac{m}{100}\right)\left(1-\tfrac{d}{100}\right)$
**Given.** Markup m = 60%, discount d = 25%.
**Working.** $f=\left(1+\tfrac{60}{100}\right)\left(1-\tfrac{25}{100}\right)=1.2$.
**Answer.** Profit $=(f-1)\times100=20\%$.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A number divided by 6 gives quotient 13 and remainder 4. The number is:
questions.solution: Number = divisor × quotient + remainder = 6×13+4 = 82.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The average age of 5 persons is 30 years. If a person aged 38 leaves, the average of the remaining 4 is:
questions.solution: Total = 150; remaining = 150 − 38 = 112; average = 28.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A and B invest in the ratio 4 : 5. Out of a total profit of Rs 10800, A's share is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A man goes at 40 km/h and returns over the same route at 60 km/h. His average speed is:
questions.solution: Average speed = 2uv/(u+v) = 2×40×60/(40+60) = 48 km/h.

questions.stem: If 60% of a number is 90, the number is:

questions.stem: A 120 m long train crosses a pole in 12 seconds. Its speed is:

questions.stem: Simple interest on Rs 6000 at 9% per annum for 5 years is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: 3 kg of rice at Rs 30/kg is mixed with 3 kg at Rs 60/kg. The average price per kg is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The percentage increase when a value rises from 60 to 72 is:

questions.stem: Pipe A fills a tank in 10 h and pipe B in 3 h. Together they fill it in:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: What is 15% of 640?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A car covers a distance at 45 km/h in 6 hours. The distance is:
questions.solution: Distance = speed × time = 45 × 6 = 270 km.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The square root of 3025 is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The HCF of 24 and 60 is:
questions.solution: The highest common factor of 24 and 60 is 12.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A can do a work in 15 days and B in 20 days. Working together they finish it in:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term in the series: 3, 7, 15, 31, 63, ?
questions.solution: Each term = previous × 2 + 1, so the next term = 63 × 2 + 1 = 127.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: In a certain code, each letter of a word is moved two steps forward in the English alphabet. The code for 'TREE' is:
questions.solution: Replace every letter by the one two places ahead: TREE → VTGG.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: If A = 1, B = 2, … , Z = 26, what is the sum of the letter values of 'ACE'?
questions.solution: Add the positional values of A, C, E to get 9.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A man walks 9 km towards north, then turns east and walks 12 km. How far is he from the starting point?
questions.solution: Straight-line distance = √(9² + 12²) = √225 = 15 km.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A is the brother of B. B is the brother of C. C is the father of D. How is A related to D?
questions.solution: Tracing each relationship step by step gives: Uncle.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: No A is B. All B are C. Which conclusion necessarily follows?
questions.solution: Applying the rules of categorical syllogism, the valid conclusion is: Some C are not A.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term in the series: 2, 5, 11, 23, 47, ?
questions.solution: Each term = previous × 2 + 1, so the next term = 47 × 2 + 1 = 95.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: In a certain code, each letter of a word is moved two steps forward in the English alphabet. The code for 'LAMP' is:
questions.solution: Replace every letter by the one two places ahead: LAMP → NCOR.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Pointing to a man, a woman said, 'His mother is the only daughter of my mother.' How is the woman related to the man?
questions.solution: The only daughter of her mother is the woman herself, so she is the man's mother.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: 2 : 4 :: 5 : ? (pattern n : n^2)

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: 4, 7, 13, 22, ?
questions.solution: Differences grow by 3; adding the next difference gives 34.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Which of these fractions is the largest?
questions.solution: Comparing decimal values, 4/5 is the largest.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A man walks 9 km north, then 12 km east. His distance from the start is:
questions.solution: Resultant = √(9² + 12²) = 15 km.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: If 2 January is a Thursday, what day is 9 January?
questions.solution: 9 − 2 = 7 days, exactly one week, so it is again Thursday.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: 16, 25, 36, 49, ?
questions.solution: These are perfect squares; next = 8² = 64.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: DE, GH, JK, ?
questions.solution: Each pair skips one letter; after JK comes MN.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.solution: Each number is halved, so 16 → 8.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: Z, X, V, T, ?
questions.solution: Letters decrease by 2 each time, so the next letter is R.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Which is the odd one out: 3, 7, 19, 25?
questions.solution: 3, 7, 19 are prime; 25 is composite.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: 5, 15, 45, 135, ?
questions.solution: Each term is multiplied by 3, so next = 135 × 3 = 405.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Hand : Glove :: Foot : ?
questions.solution: Just as Glove relates to Hand, Sock relates to Foot.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: If each letter is replaced by the next letter, BIRD is coded as:
questions.solution: Shift each letter forward by one to get CJSE.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A person facing south turns 90° to the left. Now they face:
questions.solution: Turning left from south leads to East.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: In a row of 20 people, X is 9th from the left. X's position from the right is:
questions.solution: Position from right = 20 − 9 + 1 = 12.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: The angle between the hands of a clock at 3:00 is:
questions.solution: Each hour = 30°; at 3:00 the hands are 3 × 30 = 90° apart.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: One who studies stars and celestial bodies is an:
questions.solution: An 'astronomer' scientifically studies celestial bodies.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: One word for 'a speech made without preparation':
questions.solution: An unprepared speech is delivered 'extempore'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: A place where birds are kept is called:
questions.solution: An 'aviary' houses birds; an apiary keeps bees.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The idiom 'to break the ice' means:
questions.solution: 'Break the ice' means to initiate conversation in a tense or new setting.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The idiom 'a piece of cake' means:
questions.solution: 'A piece of cake' means something very easy to do.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The idiom 'once in a blue moon' means:
questions.solution: 'Once in a blue moon' means something happening very rarely.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The idiom 'to bury the hatchet' means:
questions.solution: 'Bury the hatchet' means to end a quarrel and make peace.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The idiom 'to let the cat out of the bag' means:
questions.solution: It means to accidentally reveal a secret.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The plural of 'Child' is:
questions.solution: 'Child' has the irregular plural 'children'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The plural of 'Mouse' is:
questions.solution: 'Mouse' has the irregular plural 'mice'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'Neither he nor I ___ wrong.'
questions.solution: With 'neither...nor', the verb agrees with the nearer subject 'I', so 'am'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'Each of the students ___ a book.'
questions.solution: 'Each' is singular, so it takes 'has'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'He is ___ honest man.'
questions.solution: 'Honest' starts with a vowel sound, so 'an' is used.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'She plays ___ violin beautifully.'
questions.solution: Musical instruments take the definite article 'the'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Passive voice of 'John wrote the letter.' is:
options: The letter is written by John
The letter was written by John
questions.solution: Past simple active becomes 'was written' in the passive.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Passive voice of 'She is reading a book.' is:
options: A book is being read by her
A book is read by her
A book has been read by her
A book was read by her
questions.solution: Present continuous active becomes 'is being read' in the passive.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Spot the error: 'He / don't / like / coffee.'
questions.solution: Third-person singular needs 'doesn't', not 'don't'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Spot the error: 'One of the boys / are / playing / outside.'
questions.solution: 'One of the boys' is singular, so it requires 'is'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the correct form: 'I ___ my homework already.'
questions.solution: 'Already' signals present perfect, so 'have done' is correct.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the correct form: 'By next year, she ___ here for a decade.'
questions.solution: A completed action by a future point uses the future perfect 'will have worked'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the word that means the opposite of 'Artificial'.
questions.solution: 'Artificial' is opposite to 'natural'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the correct meaning of the word 'Frugal'.
questions.solution: 'Frugal' means careful with money, i.e. economical.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the synonym of 'Abundant'.
questions.solution: 'Abundant' means existing in large quantity, i.e. plentiful.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the synonym of 'Brief'.
questions.solution: 'Brief' means short or concise.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the synonym of 'Happy'.
questions.solution: 'Happy' is synonymous with 'joyful'.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about a singly linked list is correct?
options: Its size must be fixed at creation
It does not support O(1) random access by index
questions.solution: Linked lists require O(n) traversal to reach an arbitrary index — no random access.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Thrashing in a virtual-memory system refers to:
options: Excessive paging that cripples useful work
questions.solution: When processes spend more time swapping pages than computing, the system thrashes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Interpreted as an 8-bit two's-complement number, the bit pattern 11111010 equals:
questions.solution: MSB = 1 ⇒ negative; value = 250 − 256 = -6.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A pointer variable stores:
options: The memory address of another variable
questions.solution: Pointers hold addresses, enabling indirect access and dynamic structures.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A link of 1 Mbps has a round-trip time of 50 ms. The bandwidth-delay product is about:
questions.solution: BDP = bandwidth×delay = 1Mbps×50ms ÷ 8 ≈ 6.1 KB.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Bubble sort has a worst-case time complexity of:
questions.solution: Bubble sort makes ~n²/2 comparisons in the worst case.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Compared with a process, a thread:
options: Shares the address space of its process
Has its own copy of global data
Has a completely separate address space
questions.solution: Threads of a process share code, data and heap, but have their own stack and registers.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A data word of 48 bits occupies how many bytes?
questions.solution: 48 bits ÷ 8 = 6 bytes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A primary key in a relation must be:
questions.solution: A primary key uniquely identifies each tuple and cannot be null.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 3, push 9, push 20, pop, push 21. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 20 then pop (removes 20), pushing 21 leaves 21 on top (LIFO).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement comparing compilation and interpretation is correct?
options: Compiled code generally runs faster than interpreted code
questions.solution: Ahead-of-time compiled native code typically executes faster than line-by-line interpretation.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Polymorphism in OOP allows:
options: One interface to take many forms
questions.solution: Polymorphism lets the same call behave differently depending on the object's type.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A 200×80 array of 8-byte elements occupies how much memory?
questions.solution: Size = rows×cols×bytes = 200×80×8 = 128000 B = 125 KB.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Dijkstra's algorithm finds shortest paths correctly only when edge weights are:
questions.solution: Dijkstra assumes non-negative weights; negative edges require Bellman–Ford.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In the graph shown (edges A–B, A–C, B–D, C–D), a breadth-first search from A, visiting neighbours alphabetically, gives the order:
questions.solution: BFS from A visits A, then its neighbours B and C, then D: A, B, C, D.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A relation in BCNF is also in 3NF, but the converse may fail when:
options: A non-key attribute determines part of a candidate key
questions.solution: BCNF is stricter: every determinant must be a superkey; 3NF allows certain prime-attribute dependencies.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement is correct?
options: Any Boolean function can be built using only NAND gates
questions.solution: NAND (and NOR) are the universal gates; AND/OR/XOR alone are not functionally complete.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Binary search on a sorted array of 128 elements needs at most how many comparisons?
questions.solution: Worst-case comparisons ≈ ⌈log₂ 128⌉ = 7.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A compiler differs from an interpreter in that it:
options: Translates the whole program before execution
Executes line by line at run time
questions.solution: A compiler produces object/machine code ahead of time; an interpreter executes statements directly.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A link of 100 Mbps has a round-trip time of 100 ms. The bandwidth-delay product is about:
questions.solution: BDP = bandwidth×delay = 100Mbps×100ms ÷ 8 ≈ 1220.7 KB.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: To find shortest paths when some edge weights are negative (but no negative cycle), use:
questions.solution: Bellman–Ford handles negative edges and detects negative cycles; Dijkstra cannot.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An index on a database column generally:
options: Speeds up reads but can slow down writes
Speeds up both reads and writes equally
questions.solution: Indexes accelerate lookups but add overhead to inserts/updates that must maintain them.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: How many address lines are needed to address 16K memory locations?
questions.solution: 16K = 16384 = 2^14, so 14 address lines are required.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The ACID properties of a transaction stand for:
questions.solution: ACID guarantees reliable transaction processing.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The decimal value of the binary number 1011₂ is:
questions.solution: 1011₂ = 11₁₀ (sum of place values).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The portion of code where a process accesses shared resources is the:
questions.solution: Mutual exclusion must be enforced over the critical section to avoid race conditions.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which condition is NOT one of the four necessary conditions for deadlock?
questions.solution: Deadlock needs mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, NO preemption, and circular wait — so 'preemption' is not a condition.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The maximum number of comparisons made by bubble sort on 10 elements is:
questions.solution: Bubble sort makes n(n−1)/2 = 10×9/2 = 45 comparisons.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Belady's anomaly — more frames causing more page faults — can occur with which replacement policy?
questions.solution: FIFO can exhibit Belady's anomaly; stack algorithms like LRU and OPT cannot.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 3, push 8, push 12, pop, push 25. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 12 then pop (removes 12), pushing 25 leaves 25 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">25</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">8</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">3</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Every recursive function must have a:
questions.solution: Without a base (terminating) case, recursion never stops and overflows the stack.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: RAM is described as volatile memory because it:
options: Loses its contents when power is removed
questions.solution: Volatile memory needs power to keep data; RAM clears on power-off.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 5, push 9, push 12, pop, push 21. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 12 then pop (removes 12), pushing 21 leaves 21 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">21</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">9</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">5</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Two-phase locking (2PL) guarantees:
questions.solution: 2PL (growing then shrinking phase) ensures serializability, though deadlocks are still possible.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: With a page size of 8 KB, the number of bits used for the page offset is:
questions.solution: 8KB = 2^13 bytes, so the offset field is 13 bits.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A queue follows the ordering principle:
questions.solution: Elements leave a queue in the same order they entered — FIFO.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A problem that is NP-complete is:
options: In NP and at least as hard as every NP problem
Solvable in polynomial time for sure
questions.solution: NP-complete = in NP and NP-hard; a polynomial solution to one would solve all of NP.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the binary search tree shown (root 40), the in-order traversal sequence is:
questions.solution: In-order (left–root–right) of a BST gives ascending order: 12, 20, 28, 40, 65.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 260 150" width="260" height="150"><line x1="130" y1="30" x2="80" y2="75" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="130" y1="30" x2="190" y2="75" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="80" y1="75" x2="50" y2="120" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="80" y1="75" x2="110" y2="120" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="130" cy="30" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="130" y="34" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">40</text><circle cx="80" cy="75" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="80" y="79" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">20</text><circle cx="190" cy="75" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="190" y="79" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">65</text><circle cx="50" cy="120" r="16" fill="#dcfce7" stroke="#16a34a"/><text x="50" y="124" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">12</text><circle cx="110" cy="120" r="16" fill="#dcfce7" stroke="#16a34a"/><text x="110" y="124" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">28</text></svg>
figure.caption: Binary search tree

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cache memory is used because it is:
options: Faster than main memory, reducing access time
questions.solution: Cache is small, fast memory holding frequently used data near the CPU.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An 10-bit register can represent how many distinct values?
questions.solution: 10 bits give 2^10 = 1024 distinct patterns.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement is INCORRECT?
options: Binary search runs in O(log n)
Binary search works on an unsorted array
Linear search works on unsorted arrays
Binary search requires a sorted array
questions.solution: Binary search only works on sorted data — the claim that it works unsorted is false.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Virtual memory allows a program to:
options: Use more address space than physical RAM
questions.solution: Virtual memory backs part of the address space on disk, giving the illusion of large RAM.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Naive recursive Fibonacci fib(7) (with fib(0)=0, fib(1)=1) makes how many calls to the base cases fib(0) or fib(1)?
questions.solution: The number of base-case calls for fib(n) equals fib(n+1) = 21, illustrating exponential blow-up.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Inserting n elements into a dynamic array that doubles on resize has an amortized insertion cost of:
questions.solution: Doubling makes total work O(n) over n inserts, i.e. amortized O(1) each.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the AND gate shown with inputs A = 1 and B = 1, the output is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 110" width="220" height="110"><line x1="10" y1="35" x2="70" y2="35" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="75" x2="70" y2="75" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="6" y="30" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">A=1</text><text x="6" y="92" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">B=1</text><path d="M70 20 L110 20 Q150 55 110 90 L70 90 Z" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="95" y="60" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">AND</text><line x1="150" y1="55" x2="200" y2="55" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="48" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">?</text></svg>
figure.caption: AND gate

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A foreign key enforces:
questions.solution: A foreign key must match a primary key in the referenced table (or be null), enforcing referential integrity.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A semaphore is primarily used for:
questions.solution: Semaphores coordinate access to shared resources, preventing race conditions.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the OR gate shown with inputs A = 1 and B = 0, the output is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 110" width="220" height="110"><line x1="10" y1="35" x2="70" y2="35" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="75" x2="70" y2="75" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="6" y="30" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">A=1</text><text x="6" y="92" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">B=0</text><path d="M70 20 L110 20 Q150 55 110 90 L70 90 Z" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="95" y="60" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">OR</text><line x1="150" y1="55" x2="200" y2="55" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="48" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">?</text></svg>
figure.caption: OR gate

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: DNS is responsible for:
options: Translating domain names to IP addresses
questions.solution: The Domain Name System resolves human-readable names to IP addresses.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Transferring 500 MB at 250 MB/s takes about:
questions.solution: time = size/rate = 500/250 = 2 s.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The NAND and NOR gates are called universal gates because they can:
questions.solution: Any logic function can be built using only NAND (or only NOR) gates.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In 8-bit two's complement, the range of representable integers is:
questions.solution: 8-bit two's complement spans −2⁷ to 2⁷ − 1 = −128 to +127.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A perfect binary tree of height 6 (root at height 1) has how many nodes?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Encapsulation in object-oriented programming refers to:
options: Bundling data with the methods that operate on it
questions.solution: Encapsulation groups state and behaviour and restricts direct access to internals.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Borrowing 3 bits from the host portion of a class-C network yields how many subnets, each with how many usable hosts?
questions.solution: Subnets = 2^3 = 8; host bits = 5 ⇒ 2^5 − 2 = 30 hosts each.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: UDP, in contrast to TCP, is:
questions.solution: UDP sends datagrams without setup, acknowledgements or guaranteed delivery.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A router operates principally at the OSI:
questions.solution: Routers forward packets using IP addresses at the network layer.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Memory access = 100 ns, page-fault rate = 0.002, fault service = 10 ms. The effective access time is about:
questions.solution: EAT = (1−p)·ma + p·service = 1×100 + 0.002×10ms ≈ 20099.8 ns (faults dominate).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Inserting a node at the front of a singly linked list takes time:
questions.solution: Only a couple of pointer updates are needed — constant time.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An ideal 4-stage pipeline processes 1000 instructions. The speedup over a non-pipelined CPU is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Normalization of a database is performed mainly to:
options: Reduce redundancy and avoid update anomalies
questions.solution: Normalization organizes data to minimize duplication and anomalies.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The OSI reference model has how many layers?
questions.solution: OSI: Physical, Data-link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The decimal value of the hexadecimal number 1F₁₆ is:
questions.solution: 1F₁₆ = 31₁₀.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In an n-bit two's-complement representation, the number of distinct values is:
questions.solution: n bits encode 2ⁿ distinct values, from −2ⁿ⁻¹ to 2ⁿ⁻¹ − 1.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The truth table shown (output Y) corresponds to which logic function of A and B?
questions.solution: Reading the output column identifies the function as XOR.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 170 150" width="170" height="150"><text x="26" y="35" font-size="11" font-weight="bold">A</text><text x="66" y="35" font-size="11" font-weight="bold">B</text><text x="112" y="35" font-size="11" font-weight="bold">Y</text><line x1="20" y1="40" x2="150" y2="40" stroke="#475569"/><text x="30" y="55" font-size="11">0</text><text x="70" y="55" font-size="11">0</text><text x="120" y="55" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">0</text><text x="30" y="75" font-size="11">0</text><text x="70" y="75" font-size="11">1</text><text x="120" y="75" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">1</text><text x="30" y="95" font-size="11">1</text><text x="70" y="95" font-size="11">0</text><text x="120" y="95" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">1</text><text x="30" y="115" font-size="11">1</text><text x="70" y="115" font-size="11">1</text><text x="120" y="115" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">0</text></svg>
figure.caption: Truth table

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In round-robin CPU scheduling, each process is given:
options: A fixed time quantum in turn
questions.solution: Round-robin cycles through ready processes, each running for one time quantum.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Searching a skewed (degenerate) binary search tree of n nodes takes, in the worst case:
questions.solution: A fully skewed BST behaves like a linked list, giving O(n) search.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Transferring 100 MB at 50 MB/s takes about:
questions.solution: time = size/rate = 100/50 = 2 s.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A flip-flop is a circuit that can store:
questions.solution: A flip-flop is a basic 1-bit memory element.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cache access = 20 ns, memory access = 200 ns, hit ratio = 0.8. The effective access time is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which comparison of sorting algorithms is correct?
options: Merge sort is stable; standard quicksort is not
Quicksort is stable; merge sort is not
questions.solution: Merge sort preserves equal-key order (stable); typical quicksort does not.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Accessing the i-th element of an array by index takes time:
questions.solution: Arrays support constant-time random access via base address + index×size.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the NOR gate shown with inputs A = 0 and B = 1, the output is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 110" width="220" height="110"><line x1="10" y1="35" x2="70" y2="35" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="75" x2="70" y2="75" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="6" y="30" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">A=0</text><text x="6" y="92" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">B=1</text><path d="M70 20 L110 20 Q150 55 110 90 L70 90 Z" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="95" y="60" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">NOR</text><line x1="150" y1="55" x2="200" y2="55" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="48" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">?</text></svg>
figure.caption: NOR gate

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about greedy algorithms is correct?
options: They do not always yield a globally optimal solution
They are the same as dynamic programming
questions.solution: Greedy choices are optimal only for problems with the greedy-choice property (e.g. MST); not in general.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A 50×20 array of 8-byte elements occupies how much memory?
questions.solution: Size = rows×cols×bytes = 50×20×8 = 8000 B = 7.81 KB.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which logic gate outputs 1 only when all inputs are 1?
questions.solution: AND gives 1 only when every input is 1.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Paging is a memory-management scheme that maps:
questions.solution: Paging divides memory into fixed-size pages/frames and uses a page table to map them.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A hash table with 50 slots holds 30 keys. Its load factor is:
questions.solution: Load factor α = n/m = 30/50 = 0.6.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The worst-case time complexity of quicksort is:
questions.solution: With consistently poor pivots (e.g. already-sorted input) quicksort degrades to O(n²).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the binary search tree shown (root 60), the in-order traversal sequence is:
questions.solution: In-order (left–root–right) of a BST gives ascending order: 37, 45, 50, 60, 85.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 260 150" width="260" height="150"><line x1="130" y1="30" x2="80" y2="75" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="130" y1="30" x2="190" y2="75" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="80" y1="75" x2="50" y2="120" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="80" y1="75" x2="110" y2="120" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="130" cy="30" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="130" y="34" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">60</text><circle cx="80" cy="75" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="80" y="79" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">45</text><circle cx="190" cy="75" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="190" y="79" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">85</text><circle cx="50" cy="120" r="16" fill="#dcfce7" stroke="#16a34a"/><text x="50" y="124" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">37</text><circle cx="110" cy="120" r="16" fill="#dcfce7" stroke="#16a34a"/><text x="110" y="124" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">50</text></svg>
figure.caption: Binary search tree

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The base (radix) of the hexadecimal number system is:
questions.solution: Hexadecimal uses 16 symbols (0–9, A–F).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: B-trees are preferred over binary search trees for on-disk databases because they:
options: Are shallow (high fan-out), reducing disk accesses
questions.solution: High branching factor keeps B-trees shallow, minimizing expensive disk seeks.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The decimal value of the hexadecimal number 40₁₆ is:
questions.solution: 40₁₆ = 64₁₀.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Quicksort attains its O(n²) worst case when:
options: The pivot is always the median
The array is large but random
The pivot is consistently the smallest or largest element
questions.solution: Maximally unbalanced partitions (e.g. sorted input with first/last pivot) give O(n²).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A perfect binary tree of height 5 (root at height 1) has how many nodes?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The well-known TCP port for unencrypted HTTP is:
questions.solution: HTTP uses port 80; HTTPS uses 443.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: TCP provides a service that is:
questions.solution: TCP uses handshakes, acknowledgements and retransmission for reliable, ordered delivery.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: TLB access = 10 ns, memory access = 200 ns, TLB hit ratio = 0.9 (paged memory). The effective access time is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In the Von Neumann architecture, instructions and data:
questions.solution: Von Neumann machines store programs and data in one common memory (Harvard uses separate).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A /29 IPv4 subnet provides how many usable host addresses?
questions.solution: Host bits = 32 − 29 = 3; usable = 2^3 − 2 = 6.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about virtual memory is INCORRECT?
options: A page fault may load a page from disk
Virtual memory can exceed physical RAM
Page faults are handled entirely by the CPU without disk access
The page table maps pages to frames
questions.solution: A page fault for a non-resident page requires the OS to fetch it from disk.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The SQL clause used to retrieve data from a table is:
questions.solution: SELECT … FROM … queries rows from one or more tables.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 7, push 9, push 20, pop, push 21. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 20 then pop (removes 20), pushing 21 leaves 21 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">21</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">9</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">7</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A switch (LAN) forwards frames using:
options: MAC addresses at the data-link layer
Port numbers at the transport layer
IP addresses at the network layer
questions.solution: A Layer-2 switch learns and forwards by MAC address.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the AND gate shown with inputs A = 0 and B = 1, the output is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 110" width="220" height="110"><line x1="10" y1="35" x2="70" y2="35" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="75" x2="70" y2="75" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="6" y="30" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">A=0</text><text x="6" y="92" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">B=1</text><path d="M70 20 L110 20 Q150 55 110 90 L70 90 Z" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="95" y="60" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">AND</text><line x1="150" y1="55" x2="200" y2="55" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="48" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">?</text></svg>

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Banker's algorithm is used for deadlock:
questions.solution: It checks whether granting a request leaves the system in a safe state (avoidance).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A stack follows the ordering principle:
questions.solution: The most recently pushed element is the first to be popped — LIFO.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Demand paging brings a page into memory:
options: Only when it is first referenced (causing a page fault)
At program load time, all at once
questions.solution: Pages are loaded lazily on first access; the page fault triggers the load.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: How many address lines are needed to address 8K memory locations?
questions.solution: 8K = 8192 = 2^13, so 13 address lines are required.
